title: Chiponde Zone receives a donation
author: Mussa Kabichi
body: Chiponde LEA Primary School in Chiponde Zone, Mangochi District, has today received a donation of 30 headsets from businessman and community member Sledge Kananji, fulfilling a pledge he made during a school showcase held earlier this year. The handover ceremony follows an appeal made on March 11, 2026, by the school’s headteacher, Peter Gomire, who called on well-wishers to support the institution with additional learning equipment, particularly headsets, due to increased enrolment. At the time, Gomire highlighted that the school had 2,557 learners against only 23 teachers, a situation that has put pressure on teaching and learning resources, especially those used under the BeFIT digital learning program. Responding to the appeal, Kananji, who is also a representative of Chiponde Market traders,pledged to donate 30 headsets to help improve learners’ access to the digital lessons. Speaking during today’s handover, Kananji reaffirmed his commitment to supporting the school, stating that he remains ready to assist whenever possible to improve education standards in the area. Headteacher Gomire expressed gratitude for the timely support, describing the donation as a significant boost to the school’s efforts in delivering effective digital learning. “This support has come at the right time and will greatly help our learners benefit fully from the BeFIT program,” he said. He further encouraged other well-wishers and members of the community to emulate the gesture and contribute towards addressing the school’s resource challenges. BeFIT is a Government of Malawi-owned and led program aimed at enhancing foundational literacy and numeracy skills among learners in Standards 1 to 4 across all public primary schools in Malawi through supplemental, individualized, self-paced instruction delivered via specialized apps on Android tablets

title: Unduna wa Maphunziro wapereka njinga zamoto
body: Unduna wa Maphunziro wapereka njinga zamoto zokwana 114, zamtengo wa pafupifupi K1.5 biliyoni, kwa alangizi a maphunziro a pulayimale pofuna kulimbikitsa kuwunika kwa masukulu komanso kukweza mlingo wa maphunziro m’dziko muno. Mwambo wopereka njingazo wachitikira ku Lilongwe Lachiwiri, pomwe nduna ya Maphunziro, Sayansi ndi Ukadaulo, Bright Msaka, inanena kuti kusowa kwa mayendedwe kwakhala kukulepheretsa alangizi a maphunziro kugwira ntchito yawo moyenera, makamaka m’masukulu a pulayimale. Iye anafotokoza kuti njinga zamotozi zithandiza ma PEA kufika mosavuta ku masukulu ambiri, kuphatikizapo omwe ali m’madera akutali, zomwe zidzalimbikitse ntchito yowunika ndi kuthandiza aphunzitsi. “Takhala tikukumana ndi mavuto a mayendedwe kwa nthawi yaitali, choncho tapereka njinga zimenezi kuti zithandize kufika kumadera ovuta komanso kukonza ntchito zathu,” adatero Msaka. Iye analimbikitsanso olandira njingazo kuti azisamalira bwino ndi kuzigwiritsa ntchito pa ntchito zokhazo za boma. Akatswiri pankhani ya maphunziro akukhulupirira kuti njinga zamotozi zidzathandiza kwambiri kupititsa patsogolo gawo la maphunziro m’dziko lonse. Izi zidzachitika chifukwa alangizi a maphunziro a pulayimale adzatha kufika kumasukulu ambiri munthawi yake, kupereka chithandizo chofunikira kwa aphunzitsi, komanso kuonetsetsa kuti maphunziro akuyenda bwino, zomwe zingabweretse phindu lalikulu kwa ophunzira ndi chitukuko cha dziko.

title: Mabungwe akuluakulu a ogwira ntchito m’boma akana kuti bungwe la Teachers Union of Malawi ndi Civil Servants Trade Union ndilo lowayimilira
body:
Wolemba: Mussa Kabichi-Mangochi | 20/04/26 

Mabungwe akuluakulu a ogwira ntchito m’boma akana kuti bungwe la Teachers Union of Malawi (TUM) ndi Civil Servants Trade Union (CSTU) ndilo lowayimilira, ndipo akhazikitsa bungwe latsopano lotchedwa Federation of Civil Service Unions (FOCSU). M’chikalata chogwirizana, mabungwe a Secondary School Teachers Union (SESTU), Teacher Educators Union (TEDU), Medical Doctors Union of Malawi (MDUM), Physician Associates Union of Malawi (PAUM), Human Resources for Health Coalition (HRHC), Concerned Teachers of Malawi (CTM) ndi Concerned Civil Servants (CCS) ati TUM ndi CSTU alephera kufunsa mamembala awo pa nkhani ya kusintha kwa malipiro ndi kagwiridwe ka  ntchito. Potengera lamulo la Labour Relations Act la chaka cha 1996, mabungwewa ati ogwira ntchito ndiwo okha ali ndi ufulu wosankha amene angawaimire.

 M’chikalatacho, madandaulo akuluakulu akuphatikizapo kuwonjezeredwa kwa malipiro kwa 10 mpaka 20 peresenti komwe akuti sikugwirizana ndi mavuto a chuma omwe alipo pano; ndalama ya tchuthi ya 25 peresenti yomwe akuti ndi yochepa kwambiri poyerekeza ndi kukwera kwa mitengo ya mafuta; komanso kusasintha kwa ndalama zoyendera ndi zina zapadera ngakhale kuti mtengo wa moyo ukukwera kwambiri.

 Chikalatacho chadzudzulanso kugula kwa hotelo ya Amaryllis ndi Public Service Pension Trust Fund, kuphatikizapo kulipira ndalama zokwana K90 biliyoni kwa Yusuf Investments ngakhale kuti Reserve Bank inalamula kuti malondawo ayimitsidwe, komanso kuchotsedwa kwa K5 biliyoni ku ndalama zomwe zinali zoletsedwa popanda kuyankhapo kwa mabungwe. Kuphatikiza apo, mabungwewa akuimba mlandu boma kuti laphwanya lamulo la Pensions Act la 2023 posunga ndalama zokwana K4 biliyoni za penshoni kwa zaka ziwiri. Bungwe latsopanoli lati likuyembekezeka kuchita mkumano wogwirizana ku Lilongwe mkati mwa sabata ino kuti likambirane njira zomwe lingatsatire.

title: STRONG TURNOUT MARKS OPENING OF TERM 3 IN MALAWI SCHOOLS
body:
By Mussa Kabichi

 Schools across Malawi have today, 20 April 2026, reopened for Term 3 of the 2025–2026 academic session, with reports from across the country indicating an encouraging turnout of learners on the very first day.

 In Mulanje District, particularly in Namphungo Zone, schools registered impressive attendance figures, with learners reporting in large numbers. Education authorities attribute the development to the impact of Free Primary Education (FPE) and increased community awareness. Messages shared in churches and mosques ahead of the opening day also played a key role in reminding parents and guardians of the school calendar. A teacher in the district, Chrisprin Chataika, welcomed the turnout, saying, “The response from learners is very encouraging.

 With Free Primary Education in place, there is no reason for any child to stay at home. We are ready to guide them to succeed this term.” In Balaka District, similar scenes were observed in M’Manga Zone, Nkhonde Zone, and Namalomba Zone, where schools also recorded high attendance. Community leaders and education stakeholders have been actively encouraging learners to return to school, especially as this is the final term of the academic year.

 A community member, Memory Lapken, emphasized the importance of education, stating, “As a community, we are making sure no child is left behind. With school fees removed, every learner has the opportunity to attend, and we are encouraging parents to take this seriously.”

 Meanwhile, in the Mangochi District, reports also indicate a strong turnout in several schools, with learners showing determination to perform well and advance to the next class. Many schools began the day with cleaning activities and orientation sessions before lessons resumed. A Parent-Teacher Association (PTA) member, Magret Injesi, expressed optimism about the term, saying, “We are impressed with how learners have reported in large numbers . This shows that both parents and learners understand the value of education, especially in this final term. As Malawi moves through the final term of the academic year, the continued success of Free Primary Education is becoming increasingly evident. Rising enrolment and improved attendance are expected to significantly reduce the number of people without an educational background, ultimately contributing to a more educated population and supporting the country’s long-term development goals.

title: Free Education Rollout Receives Mixed Reactions in Malawi
body:
By Mussa Kabichi | 16 March 2026

The government’s rollout of free education in public primary and secondary schools is receiving mixed reactions, with stakeholders welcoming increased access while raising concerns over funding and implementation.

The policy, introduced this term, has eased the financial burden on many households by removing school-related costs such as fees and contributions. At the secondary level, learners selected to government schools can now attend without paying tuition, a development widely praised by parents.

However, findings by Education Matters Online show that while the initiative has boosted enrolment, schools are struggling to cope with limited and delayed funding under the Free Primary Education (FPE) program.

Headteachers and teachers say the current allocation of about K1,000 per learner is insufficient to meet operational demands, particularly for examinations, which in some cases cost up to K2,000 per learner. They also report that funds are often disbursed late and in small portions, affecting the planning and delivery of lessons.

“FPE is a good initiative, but the funding is too little and does not come on time,” said one teacher. Others noted that some schools are failing to print examination papers due to financial constraints, forcing learners to write tests from chalkboards.

Despite these challenges, the policy has led to a noticeable increase in learner participation. Teachers report a rise in the number of pupils sitting for examinations, while many learners say they no longer miss exams due to lack of fees.

“We are happy that we can now sit for exams without paying,” said one learner, though others expressed concern over the difficulty of writing exams from the board due to limited resources.

School committees and other stakeholders have also called for increased and timely funding, emphasizing the need for clear guidelines on resource allocation and management to ensure smooth implementation.

Parents, on the other hand, have largely welcomed the initiative, describing it as a major relief. Many say their children are now able to attend school consistently without the risk of being sent home for non-payment.

While the policy marks a significant step toward expanding access to education, stakeholders say its success will depend on addressing funding gaps and improving implementation. Strengthening financial support and ensuring timely disbursement of resources will be critical to delivering not only access but also quality education across Malawi.
